text: Numerous peptide hormones adopt $\alpha$-helical conformations upon receptor binding, and the development of sensitive detection methods for these molecules is critical for improving clinical disease management. While de novo protein design has successfully produced high-affinity binders for structured proteins, designing interfaces for short peptides with high helical propensity remains a formidable challenge. In this study, we present a synergistic approach utilizing parametric generation and deep learning to overcome this limitation. By extending the RFdiffusion framework to accommodate flexible targets and implementing a "partial diffusion" strategy—which refines structural models through iterative noising and denoising—we generated binders with picomolar affinity. These binders were produced either by optimizing initial templates or entirely de novo from random noise, achieving high potency without the need for experimental maturation. We demonstrate that these RFdiffusion-designed proteins facilitate the mass spectrometry-based enrichment of parathyroid hormone and glucagon and enable the development of modular bioluminescent biosensors. The capacity to design binders for conformationally plastic targets and to refine proteins via partial diffusion represents a significant advancement in computational design with broad therapeutic and diagnostic utility.
